content.problemStatement:
MoEngage has given you an array AAA of NNN integers and a positive integer CCC.

You can apply the following operation on the array **any** number of times. In one operation:


Choose a **strictly increasing** array BBB of length MMM such that:

B1=1,BM=N,B_1 = 1, B_M = N,B1​=1,BM​=N, and 1<Bi<N1 < B_i < N1<Bi​<N for all (1<i<M)(1 < i < M)(1<i<M);
ABi>0A_{B_i} \gt 0ABi​​>0 for all (1≤i≤M)(1 \leq i \leq M)(1≤i≤M).


Subtract 111 from ABiA_{B_i}ABi​​ for all (1≤i≤M)(1 \leq i \leq M)(1≤i≤M).

The **cost** of this operation is ∑i=1(M−1)(Bi+1−Bi)2\sum_{i = 1}^{(M-1)}(B_{i + 1} - B_i) ^ 2∑i=1(M−1)​(Bi+1​−Bi​)2.
Note that, MMM can be different for different operations.

Let KKK be the total cost after performing XXX number of operations on the array. Your goal is to **maximize** the value of (C⋅X−K)(C\cdot X - K)(C⋅X−K).


Input Format

First line contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
For each of the TTT test cases, the first line contains two integers NNN and CCC.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers denoting A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2,\ldots ,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer - the maximum value of (C⋅X−K)(C\cdot X - K)(C⋅X−K).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and C. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the maximum possible value of (C·X − K), where X is the number of performed operations and K is the total cost of those operations.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Given N=3N = 3N=3, A=[3,1,5]A = [3,1,5]A=[3,1,5], C=3C = 3C=3.
It is optimal to apply only 111 operation with B=[1,2,3]B = [1,2,3]B=[1,2,3]. The cost for the operation would be (2−1)2+(3−2)2=2(2 - 1)^2 + (3 - 2)^2 = 2(2−1)2+(3−2)2=2. The value of X=1X = 1X=1 and the total cost K=2K = 2K=2. Thus, (C⋅X−K)=(3⋅1−2)=1(C \cdot X - K) = (3 \cdot 1 - 2) = 1(C⋅X−K)=(3⋅1−2)=1.
After the operation, the array becomes A=[2,0,4]A = [2, 0, 4]A=[2,0,4].


Test case 222: Given N=7N = 7N=7, A=[5,5,5,4,1,2,1]A = [5,5,5,4,1,2,1]A=[5,5,5,4,1,2,1], C=5C = 5C=5.
It can be proven that it is never optimal to apply any operation. Hence, the answer will be (5⋅0−0)=0(5\cdot 0 - 0) = 0(5⋅0−0)=0.


Test case 333: Given N=2N = 2N=2, A=[3,3]A = [3,3]A=[3,3], C=10C = 10C=10.
It is optimal to apply 333 operations on the array. In each operation, it is optimal to take B=[1,2]B = [1,2]B=[1,2]. The cost for each operation would be (2−1)2=1(2 - 1)^2 = 1(2−1)2=1. Thus, X=3X = 3X=3 and total cost K=1+1+1=3K = 1+1+1 = 3K=1+1+1=3. Thus, (C⋅X−K)=(10⋅3−3)=27(C \cdot X - K) = (10 \cdot 3 - 3) = 27(C⋅X−K)=(10⋅3−3)=27.
After all the operations, the array becomes A=[0,0]A = [0, 0]A=[0,0].

solution.approach:
For each operation we must include positions 1 and N. To minimise the cost of an operation we should include **all** positions whose current value is still positive – omitting any positive index would only increase the sum of squared gaps (because (a+b)^2 ≥ a^2+b^2). Hence an optimal sequence of operations is:
1. In every operation use the set S_t = { i | Ai ≥ t } (t = 1 … X), where X = min(A1, AN).
2. The cost of operation t is the sum of squares of distances between consecutive indices in S_t.
The total cost K = Σ_{t=1}^{X} cost_t.
We compute K efficiently by processing the operations **backwards**. Let time t run from X down to 1. When we move from t+1 to t we insert every index i (2 ≤ i ≤ N‑1) whose value becomes ≥ t, i.e. whose Ai ≥ t and Ai < t+1. In practice we insert index i at time = min(Ai, X). While inserting a position pos we locate its current predecessor p and successor s in the ordered set of alive positions, remove the old gap (s‑p)^2 from the current total cost and add the two new gaps (pos‑p)^2 and (s‑pos)^2. After all insertions for the current t are done, the current total cost equals cost_t and we add it to the answer K.
Data structures used:
* A balanced binary search tree (C++ std::set, Java TreeSet, Python's built‑in set with bisect via sorted list, etc.) to keep the alive positions ordered and to obtain predecessor/successor in O(log N).
* A vector of pairs (time, position) for all interior indices, sorted by time descending, to know which indices to insert at each step.
All arithmetic is performed with 128‑bit integers (C++ __int128, Java BigInteger, Python int, etc.) because K can be larger than 2^63.
Finally the answer for a test case is C·X − K (still using 128‑bit) and is printed as a decimal number.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case – each index is inserted once and each insertion performs O(log N) predecessor/successor queries.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the positions and the ordered set.
